algorithms.title: Universal Chain of Survival (2025) — BLS Provider View
algorithms.description: The 2025 AHA universal Chain of Survival unifies adult and pediatric, in-hospital and out-of-hospital cardiac arrest into a single six-link sequence. BLS providers own the earliest, most impactful links.
steps: Link 1 — Activation of emergency response: recognize cardiac arrest and immediately summon help, an AED, and advanced providers.
Link 2 — High-quality CPR: begin chest compressions right away, pushing hard and fast with full recoil and minimal interruptions.
Link 3 — Defibrillation: apply an AED and deliver a shock as soon as one is available for a shockable rhythm.
Link 4 — Advanced resuscitation interventions: trained providers add airway management, medications, and treatment of reversible causes.
Link 5 — Post-cardiac arrest care: after ROSC, continue monitoring, support oxygenation and circulation, and transport to definitive care.
Link 6 — Recovery: rehabilitation, psychological support, and treatment planning for the survivor and support for the family.

algorithms.title: System-of-Care Emphasis by Setting
algorithms.description: The universal chain is applied with different front-end emphasis depending on where the arrest occurs.
steps: Out-of-hospital (OHCA): emphasize bystander recognition, calling for help, dispatcher-assisted CPR, and public-access AED use before EMS arrival.
In-hospital (IHCA): emphasize surveillance, monitoring, early-warning scores, and rapid-response systems to prevent arrest before it happens.
All settings: deliver high-quality CPR and rapid defibrillation, ensure smooth hand-offs between responders, and use quality improvement to strengthen the system.
Newborns: apply the separate Neonatal Chain of Care rather than the universal chain.
